content.text: Choosing an Identity Type

Azure provides four mechanisms for authenticating non-human workloads: system-assigned managed identities, user-assigned managed identities, service principals with certificates, and service principals with client secrets. Workload Identity Federation (WIF) is a fifth option that replaces stored credentials with short-lived OIDC tokens from an external IdP. Choosing the right type depends on where the workload runs, whether identities need to be shared, and what credential management burden the team can sustain.

attrs.alt: Decision tree for choosing an Azure identity type

content.text: The Four Identity Types
Each identity type has a distinct operational profile. Understanding the trade-offs between them — credential lifetime, scope of use, and management overhead — drives the right choice.
Identity Type
Where it runs
Credential
Shareable
Best for
System-assigned MI
Azure resource
None (platform-managed)
No
Single-resource workloads with simple lifecycle
User-assigned MI
Azure resource
None (platform-managed)
Yes
Multi-resource or containerized workloads
SP + WIF
External / CI/CD
None (federated OIDC)
No*
GitHub Actions, GCP, on-prem with OIDC IdP
SP + Certificate
Any
Asymmetric key pair
Yes
Cross-cloud or legacy workloads needing strong auth
SP + Client Secret
Any
Shared secret
Yes
Last resort; short-lived automation only
*WIF federated credentials are scoped to a specific issuer/subject pair; they can authenticate to Entra ID from any host presenting a matching OIDC token.
System-Assigned Managed Identities

A system-assigned managed identity (SAMI) is created as a property of an Azure resource. Its lifecycle is bound to that resource: enabling it creates a service principal in Entra ID; deleting the resource deletes the SP automatically. There are no credentials to rotate or store.
When to use
The workload runs on a single Azure resource (VM, App Service, AKS pod via pod identity, Function App)
The identity does not need to be reused by any other resource
You want automatic cleanup when the resource is deleted
Enabling and assigning a role

# Enable on a VM
az vm identity assign \
  --name myVM \
  --resource-group myRG

# Grant Storage Blob Data Reader on a container
PRINCIPAL_ID=$(az vm identity show \
  --name myVM --resource-group myRG \
  --query principalId -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id $PRINCIPAL_ID \
  --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
  --role 'Storage Blob Data Reader' \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mysa/blobServices/default/containers/mycontainer
Retrieving a token from inside the VM

import requests

# IMDS endpoint — only reachable from within an Azure resource
IMDS = 'http://169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token'
params = {
    'api-version': '2018-02-01',
    'resource': 'https://storage.azure.com/',
}
resp = requests.get(IMDS, params=params, headers={'Metadata': 'true'})
token = resp.json()['access_token']

# Use the token to call the Storage REST API
headers = {'Authorization': f'Bearer {token}', 'x-ms-version': '2020-04-08'}
blob_url = 'https://mysa.blob.core.windows.net/mycontainer/myfile.txt'
data = requests.get(blob_url, headers=headers).content
User-Assigned Managed Identities

A user-assigned managed identity (UAMI) is a standalone Azure resource created independently of any compute resource. It can be attached to multiple VMs, App Services, or pods simultaneously, and its lifecycle is managed separately.
When to use
The same identity must be shared by multiple compute resources (e.g., all VMs in a fleet reading the same Key Vault)
Containerized workloads where containers start and stop frequently — you pre-create the identity and attach it to the node pool or pod
Blue-green deployments where the new slot must have the same permissions as the old before traffic switches
You need to manage permission assignment independently of compute resource creation order

# Create the identity
az identity create \
  --name fleet-reader \
  --resource-group myRG

CLIENT_ID=$(az identity show \
  --name fleet-reader --resource-group myRG \
  --query clientId -o tsv)

PRINCIPAL_ID=$(az identity show \
  --name fleet-reader --resource-group myRG \
  --query principalId -o tsv)

# Assign role once — applies to all attached resources
az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id $PRINCIPAL_ID \
  --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
  --role 'Key Vault Secrets User' \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/myKV

# Attach to an App Service
az webapp identity assign \
  --name myApp --resource-group myRG \
  --identities /subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.ManagedIdentity/userAssignedIdentities/fleet-reader
Selecting a specific UAMI from code
When a resource has both a SAMI and one or more UAMIs, you must specify which identity to use by passing its client ID to the SDK.

from azure.identity import ManagedIdentityCredential
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient

# Pin to the UAMI client ID — avoids ambiguity when multiple identities are attached
CLIENT_ID = '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'

credential = ManagedIdentityCredential(client_id=CLIENT_ID)
client = SecretClient(vault_url='https://myKV.vault.azure.net', credential=credential)
secret = client.get_secret('db-password').value
Service Principals with Workload Identity Federation

Workload Identity Federation (WIF) allows an external workload — a GitHub Actions job, a GCP workload, or any OIDC-capable system — to exchange its IdP-issued token for an Entra ID access token. No secret or certificate is stored in Entra ID. The federation trust is configured as a federated credential on the App Registration.
When to use
The workload runs outside Azure: GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, GCP Cloud Run, on-premises Kubernetes with an OIDC provider
You want zero stored credentials — no rotation, no secret sprawl
The external IdP issues OIDC tokens with a stable issuer URL and predictable subject claim

# Create app registration and SP
az ad app create --display-name 'gh-deploy'
APP_ID=$(az ad app list --display-name 'gh-deploy' --query '[0].appId' -o tsv)
az ad sp create --id $APP_ID

# Add federated credential for GitHub Actions
az ad app federated-credential create \
  --id $APP_ID \
  --parameters '{
    "name": "github-main",
    "issuer": "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com",
    "subject": "repo:myorg/myrepo:ref:refs/heads/main",
    "audiences": ["api://AzureADTokenExchange"]
  }'

# GitHub Actions workflow uses azure/login with client-id only (no secret)
# - uses: azure/login@v2
#   with:
#     client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
#     tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
#     subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}

WIF is the preferred approach for any CI/CD pipeline. The GitHub Actions OIDC token is short-lived (5 minutes) and scoped to the specific job, making it far safer than a long-lived client secret stored in GitHub Secrets.
Service Principals with Certificates

Certificate-based credentials use asymmetric cryptography. The private key is held by the workload; the public certificate is registered in Entra ID. During authentication, the workload signs a JWT client assertion with the private key, and Entra ID verifies it against the registered certificate. No credential is transmitted — only a signed proof of possession.
When to use
The workload runs outside Azure and an OIDC IdP is not available for WIF
Compliance requirements mandate asymmetric authentication (no shared secrets)
The credential needs to be stored in a hardware security module (HSM) or platform TPM

# Generate a self-signed cert (for dev; use a CA-issued cert for production)
openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:4096 -keyout key.pem -out cert.pem \
  -days 365 -nodes -subj '/CN=myapp-sp'

# Register the cert with the App Registration
az ad app credential reset \
  --id $APP_ID \
  --cert @cert.pem \
  --append

# Authenticate with the cert from Python
# from azure.identity import CertificateCredential
# credential = CertificateCredential(
#     tenant_id=TENANT_ID,
#     client_id=CLIENT_ID,
#     certificate_path='cert_and_key.pem'
# )
Service Principals with Client Secrets

Client secrets are shared symmetric credentials. They are the easiest to configure but carry the highest risk: secrets can be leaked, they must be rotated before expiry, and any system that holds the secret can impersonate the SP. Use client secrets only when no other option is available.
When to use (sparingly)
Quick prototyping or local development where full WIF or cert setup is impractical
Third-party SaaS integrations that only accept client_id + client_secret (no certificate or federated auth)
Short-lived automation scripts where the secret is generated, used once, then immediately deleted
Mitigation checklist when secrets are unavoidable
Store the secret in Azure Key Vault — never in source code, config files, or CI environment variables as plain text
Set the shortest expiry the platform allows (maximum 2 years; prefer 90 days or less)
Rotate the secret before expiry using a Key Vault rotation policy or automated pipeline
Enable Entra ID sign-in logs and alert on unexpected client credential usage
Restrict the SP to the minimum required roles and scopes
Quick Reference: Decision Criteria
Scenario
Recommended type
Reason
VM reading a Key Vault secret
System-assigned MI
Single resource, automatic lifecycle
Fleet of App Services sharing a storage account
User-assigned MI
Shared identity, independent lifecycle
GitHub Actions deploying to Azure
SP + WIF
No stored credential, OIDC native
GCP function calling Azure API
SP + WIF
Cross-cloud, OIDC exchange
On-prem app, no OIDC IdP, compliance requires asymmetric
SP + Certificate
Asymmetric, no secret transmitted
SaaS product that only supports client_id/secret
SP + Client Secret
Platform constraint; mitigate with KV
Common Mistakes

Using system-assigned MI for containers: container lifecycle is shorter than the resource (the node), so a SAMI on the node is shared across all containers — use UAMI with pod identity (AKS Workload Identity) to scope the credential to the pod.
Forgetting to specify client_id when multiple UAMIs are attached: the SDK picks arbitrarily; always pin with client_id=.

Treating WIF subject claims as wildcards: the subject must match exactly. repo:org/repo:ref:refs/heads/main does not match pull_request events — configure separate federated credentials for each subject.
Setting client secret expiry to 2 years and then forgetting: the secret becomes a long-lived credential with no rotation. Use 90-day expiry and automate rotation.
Over-privileging the SP because assigning fine-grained roles takes time: a Contributor-scoped SP is a blast-radius risk. Always assign the minimum required role at the minimum required scope.
AKS Workload Identity: Managed Identity for Kubernetes Pods

In Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS), the node pool may have a system-assigned or user-assigned MI, but that identity is shared by all pods on the node — a compromised pod would have access to every resource the node identity can reach. AKS Workload Identity solves this by projecting a UAMI as a Kubernetes service account token that individual pods exchange for an Entra ID token via WIF.
How it works
A UAMI is created and assigned roles (e.g., Key Vault Secrets User)
The UAMI is associated with a Kubernetes ServiceAccount using an annotation
AKS injects a projected service account token into each pod that uses that ServiceAccount
The Azure SDK inside the pod exchanges the SA token for an Entra ID access token using the WIF OIDC flow
The pod acquires the target resource token — never seeing the UAMI's underlying credential

# 1. Create a UAMI for the pods
az identity create --name pod-kv-reader --resource-group myRG
CLIENT_ID=$(az identity show --name pod-kv-reader --resource-group myRG --query clientId -o tsv)
PRINCIPAL_ID=$(az identity show --name pod-kv-reader --resource-group myRG --query principalId -o tsv)

# 2. Grant Key Vault access
az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id $PRINCIPAL_ID \
  --role 'Key Vault Secrets User' \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/myKV

# 3. Enable OIDC issuer and Workload Identity on the AKS cluster
az aks update \
  --name myCluster --resource-group myRG \
  --enable-oidc-issuer \
  --enable-workload-identity

# 4. Get the OIDC issuer URL
ISSUER=$(az aks show --name myCluster --resource-group myRG --query oidcIssuerProfile.issuerUrl -o tsv)

# 5. Create a Kubernetes ServiceAccount annotated with the UAMI client ID
kubectl create namespace myapp
kubectl annotate serviceaccount default \
  --namespace myapp \
  azure.workload.identity/client-id=$CLIENT_ID

# 6. Create the federated credential linking AKS OIDC issuer -> UAMI
az identity federated-credential create \
  --name aks-myapp \
  --identity-name pod-kv-reader \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --issuer $ISSUER \
  --subject system:serviceaccount:myapp:default \
  --audiences api://AzureADTokenExchange

# Pod manifest — label triggers token projection
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: kv-reader
  namespace: myapp
  labels:
    azure.workload.identity/use: 'true'
spec:
  serviceAccountName: default
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myacr.azurecr.io/kv-reader:latest
    env:
    - name: AZURE_CLIENT_ID
      value: '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'  # UAMI client ID
    - name: AZURE_TENANT_ID
      value: '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'

# Inside the pod — WorkloadIdentityCredential picks up the projected SA token
from azure.identity import WorkloadIdentityCredential
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient

# No client_id needed — read from AZURE_CLIENT_ID env var injected by AKS
credential = WorkloadIdentityCredential()
client = SecretClient(vault_url='https://myKV.vault.azure.net', credential=credential)
value = client.get_secret('api-key').value

AKS Workload Identity is the recommended pattern for containerised workloads. It gives each logical application a distinct identity with its own minimal role assignments, eliminating node-level blast radius.
Auditing Identity Usage with KQL
Azure Monitor and Entra ID logs expose which identity authenticated, when, and from where. Querying these logs lets you audit compliance, detect anomalies, and identify credentials nearing expiry.
Track SP logins by credential type

// Entra ID sign-in logs: service principal logins, grouped by credential type
AADServicePrincipalSignInLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| extend CredType = iff(ServicePrincipalCredentialKeyId != '', 'Certificate/WIF', 'ClientSecret')
| summarize LoginCount=count(), LastSeen=max(TimeGenerated)
    by AppDisplayName, ServicePrincipalId, CredType
| order by LastSeen desc
Detect client secret logins (should be rare)

// Alert: any SP authenticated with a client secret in the last 24h
AADServicePrincipalSignInLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(24h)
| where ServicePrincipalCredentialKeyId == ''  // empty = client secret
| project TimeGenerated, AppDisplayName, ServicePrincipalId, IPAddress, Location
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Find managed identity token acquisitions

// Managed identity IMDS calls surfaced in AADManagedIdentitySignInLogs
AADManagedIdentitySignInLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(24h)
| summarize TokenCount=count(), Resources=make_set(ResourceDisplayName)
    by ServicePrincipalName, ConditionalAccessStatus
| order by TokenCount desc
Automating Identity Governance
At scale, manual identity hygiene does not hold. These patterns automate credential expiry alerts, orphaned SP detection, and periodic access reviews.
Alert on expiring SP credentials

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# List SP credentials expiring in the next 30 days
THRESHOLD=$(date -u -d '+30 days' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)

az ad app list --all --query '[].{AppId:appId, Name:displayName}' -o json | jq -r '.[].AppId' | while read APP_ID; do
  az ad app credential list --id $APP_ID -o json 2>/dev/null | jq -r \
    --arg app "$APP_ID" --arg threshold "$THRESHOLD" '
    .[] | select(.endDateTime < $threshold)
    | "EXPIRING: app=\($app) keyId=\(.keyId) type=\(.type) expires=\(.endDateTime)"'
done
Detect service principals with no recent activity

// SPs registered in Entra ID but with no sign-in in 90 days
// Cross-reference App registrations list with sign-in logs
let ActiveSPs = AADServicePrincipalSignInLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(90d)
| summarize LastLogin=max(TimeGenerated) by ServicePrincipalId;
AuditLogs
| where OperationName == 'Add service principal'
| extend SPId = tostring(TargetResources[0].id)
| join kind=leftanti ActiveSPs on $left.SPId == $right.ServicePrincipalId
| project TimeGenerated, SPId, InitiatedBy=tostring(InitiatedBy.user.userPrincipalName)
| order by TimeGenerated asc
Terraform: enforce no client secrets via Azure Policy

Governance at scale requires automation. Combine expiry alerts (bash/KQL) with access reviews (Entra ID Identity Governance) and Policy-level deny rules to prevent new client secrets from being added without exception approval.
Summary

Prefer managed identities for workloads running inside Azure — they eliminate credential management entirely. For external workloads, Workload Identity Federation is the modern answer: zero stored credentials, short-lived tokens, and no rotation burden. Fall back to certificate credentials when WIF is unavailable and an asymmetric approach is required. Reserve client secrets for situations where the platform leaves no other choice, and mitigate their risk with Key Vault storage, short expiry, and automated rotation.
